---
title: "`scan1gen` examples"
output:
    html_document:
        toc: true
        toc_depth: 2
        toc_float: true
---

```{r knitr_opts, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.width=9, fig.height=5, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE)
options(width=127, digits=5)

# make sure packages are available
library(qtl)
library(qtl2)
library(pscl)
library(regress)
library(sommer)
```

In [R/qtl2](https://kbroman.org/qtl2) version
[0.44](https://github.com/rqtl/qtl2/releases/tag/0.44), we introduced
the function
[`scan1gen`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qtl2/refman/qtl2.html#scan1gen)
for performing a general genome scan with a user-supplied fitting
function. We also modified
[`scan1perm`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qtl2/refman/qtl2.html#scan1perm),
for permutation tests to establish statistical significance, so that
it can take alternative scanning functions, including
[`scan1gen`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qtl2/refman/qtl2.html#scan1gen)
and
[`scan1snps`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qtl2/refman/qtl2.html#scan1snps).

In this document, we'll illustrate the use of these functions with a
few examples.


The R/qtl2 function
[`scan1`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qtl2/refman/qtl2.html#scan1)
is used to scan the genome, fitting a single-QTL model at each
location, and comparing it to a NULL model (with no QTL). The output
is a matrix of LOD scores, where the rows correspond to genomic
positions and the columns correspond to phenotypes.
But the available models are limited: you can fit a normal linear
regression model, with or without a residual polygenic effect, or for
binary traits a logistic regression model (with no residual polygenic
effect allowed).

The function
[`scan1gen`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qtl2/refman/qtl2.html#scan1gen)
helps to extend this to general phenotype models. The user provides a
function that takes as input the genotype probabilities and
phenotypes, and potentially also additive covariates, interactive
covariates, and a kinship matrix, and returns a LOD score.
The [`scan1gen`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qtl2/refman/qtl2.html#scan1gen)
function then applies this to each position in the genome.

## Generalized linear model

As a first example, let's suppose we wanted to fit a generalized
linear model for a binary phenotype, but with a probit link rather
than a logit. We could use the R function `glm()`, as in the following.
(Note that we allow just additive covariates.)

```{r ll_glm}
ll_glm <-
function(pr, pheno, addcovar=NULL, ...)
{
    formula <- ifelse(is.null(pr), "pheno ~ 1", "pheno ~ pr")
    if(!is.null(addcovar)) formula <- paste(formula, "+ addcovar")

    glm_out <- glm(as.formula(formula), family=binomial(link=probit))

    -glm_out$deviance/(2*log(10)) # log10 likelihood
}
```

Let's now use this function in `scan1gen`. First we load and prepare
the `iron` dataset included with R/qtl2.

```{r prepare_iron_data}
# load R/qtl2
library(qtl2)

# read data
iron <- read_cross2(system.file("extdata", "iron.zip", package="qtl2"))

# insert pseudomarkers into map
iron_map <- insert_pseudomarkers(iron$gmap, step=1)

# calculate genotype probabilities
iron_probs <- calc_genoprob(iron, iron_map, error_prob=0.002)

# covariates for X chr under null
Xcovar <- get_x_covar(iron)
```

The `iron` dataset includes two phenotypes; we'll turn the first into
a binary trait, 0 or 1 according to whether below or above the median.

```{r iron_binary_trait}
# create binary trait
bin_pheno <- setNames(as.numeric(iron$pheno[,1] > median(iron$pheno[,1])),
                      rownames(iron$pheno))
```

We run `scan1gen` much like `scan1`:

```{r scan1gen_glm}
out_probit <- scan1gen(iron_probs, bin_pheno, Xcovar=Xcovar, func=ll_glm, cores=0)
```

Compare this to the output of `scan1` with a logit model. The results
are very small.

```{r iron_scan1}
out_scan1 <- scan1(iron_probs, bin_pheno, Xcovar=Xcovar, model="binary")

par(mar=c(4.1,4.1,0.6,0.6))
plot(out_probit - out_scan1, iron_map, ylim=c(-0.05,0.05),
     ylab="LOD difference (probit - logit)")
```

Let's also try using `glm()` with a logit link, to see whether it
gives the same results as `scan1()`. It does.

```{r glm_logit}
ll_glm_logit <-
function(pr, pheno, addcovar=NULL, ...)
{
    formula <- ifelse(is.null(pr), "pheno ~ 1", "pheno ~ pr")
    if(!is.null(addcovar)) formula <- paste(formula, "+ addcovar")

    glm_out <- glm(as.formula(formula), family=binomial(link=logit))

    -glm_out$deviance/(2*log(10)) # log10 likelihood
}

out_logit <- scan1gen(iron_probs, bin_pheno, Xcovar=Xcovar, func=ll_glm_logit, cores=0)

par(mar=c(4.1,4.1,0.6,0.6))
plot(out_logit - out_scan1, iron_map, ylim=c(-0.05,0.05),
     ylab="LOD difference (glm - scan1)")
```


## Permutation test

To perform a permutation test, to establish statistical significance,
use the R/qtl2 function `scan1perm`. We've added an argument
`scan_func` so that `scan1perm` can be used with general scanning
functions like `scan1gen` (and also
[`scan1snps`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qtl2/refman/qtl2.html#scan1snps);
see below).

To do the permutation test, we call `scan1perm` with
`scan_func=scan1gen` and `func=ll_glm` (which gets passed along to
`scan1gen`). Here we will seek separate autosome and X-chromosome
significance thresholds.

```{r scan1perm_glm, eval=FALSE}
operm <- scan1perm(iron_probs, bin_pheno, Xcovar=Xcovar, n_perm=1000,
                   perm_Xsp=TRUE, scan_func=scan1gen, func=ll_glm,
                   chr_lengths=chr_lengths(iron_map), cores=0)
```

```{r scan1perm_glm_really, echo=FALSE}
set.seed(20260709)
rds_file <- "scan1gen_cache/glm_perms.rds"
if(!dir.exists("scan1gen_cache")) dir.create("scan1gen_cache")
if(!file.exists(rds_file)) {
<<scan1perm_glm>>
    saveRDS(operm, rds_file)
} else {
    operm <- readRDS(rds_file)
}
```

Here are the 5% significance thresholds for the autosomes and X
chromosome.

```{r summary_scan1perm_glm}
summary(operm)
```


## Zero-inflated Poisson regression

As a second example, let's do a scan with a zero-inflated Poisson
regression model, using the function `zeroinfl` in the
[pscl](https://cran.r-project.org/package=pscl) package.

Let's start with our fitting function:

```{r fit0infl}
fit0infl <-
function(probs, pheno, addcovar=NULL, ...)
{
    formula <- ifelse(is.null(probs), "pheno~1", "pheno~probs")
    if(!is.null(addcovar)) formula <- paste(formula, "+ addcovar")

    pscl::zeroinfl(as.formula(formula))$loglik/log(10)
}
```

To illustrate the function, let's simulate some F~2~ intercross data.
Our model has two QTL, with one QTL affecting the proportion of
individuals with 0 phenotype, and the other affecting the average
phenotype for those with non-zero phenotype.

```{r simulate_f2_data}
library(qtl)

set.seed(20260613)
data(map10)
n <- 250
f2 <- sim.cross(map10, model=rbind(c(2, 45, 1, 0), c(5, 65, 1, 1)),
                n.ind=n, type="f2")

f2$pheno[,1] <- rbinom(nind(f2), 1, p=c(0.8, 0.6, 0.4)[f2$qtlgeno[,1]])
f2$pheno[,2] <- f2$pheno[,1]
nonzero <- (f2$pheno[,1]>0)
f2$pheno[nonzero,2] <- rpois(sum(nonzero), c(6,4,2)[f2$qtlgeno[nonzero,2]])

f2$pheno$sex <- factor(rep("male", n))
colnames(f2$pheno) <- c("zero", "count", "sex")

f2 <- convert2cross2(f2)
```

We can run the genome scan as follows:

```{r scan1_0infl}
f2_map <- insert_pseudomarkers(f2$gmap, step=1)
f2_probs <- calc_genoprob(f2, f2_map, err=0)

out_0infl <- scan1gen(f2_probs, f2$pheno[,"count"], func=fit0infl, cores=0)
```

Here's a plot of the results:

```{r plot_scan1_0infl}
par(mar=c(5.1,4.1,0.6,0.6))
plot(out_0infl, f2_map)
```

We can perform a permutation test as we did with glm:

```{r perms_0infl, eval=FALSE}
operm_0infl <- scan1perm(f2_probs, f2$pheno[,"count"], scan_func=scan1gen,
                         func=fit0infl, cores=0, n_perm=1000)
```

```{r perms_0infl_really, echo=FALSE}
set.seed(20260710)
rds_file <- "scan1gen_cache/perms_0infl.rds"
if(!file.exists(rds_file)) {
<<perms_0infl>>
    saveRDS(operm_0infl, rds_file)
} else {
    operm_0infl <- readRDS(rds_file)
}
```

Here is the 5% significance threshold:

```{r summary_perms_0infl}
summary(operm_0infl)
```


## regress package

As a third example, let's try using the
[regress](https://cran.r-project.org/package=regress) package
to fit a polygenic model. While `scan1` in R/qtl2 estimates the
polygenic variance under the null hypothesis of no QTL, and then takes
it as fixed when scanning the genome, here we will re-estimate the
residual polygenic variance at each QTL location.

Here is our fitting function:

```{r regress_fitter}
reg <- function(probs, pheno, addcovar=NULL, kinship, ...) {
    formula <- ifelse(is.null(probs), "pheno~1", "pheno~probs")
    K <- model.matrix(~rep(1, length(pheno)))

    if(!is.null(addcovar)) formula <- paste(formula, "+ addcovar")

    regress::regress(as.formula(formula), ~kinship, kernel=K)$llik/log(10)
}
```

We'll use the `iron` dataset again. We need to calculate the kinship
matrix. We can try either the overall matrix, or use "loco" (leave one
chromosome out).

```{r iron_kinship}
k <- calc_kinship(iron_probs)
k_loco <- calc_kinship(iron_probs, "loco")
```

We'll now do the genome scan four ways: with `scan1` or regress, and
with the overall kinship or the "loco" kinship.

```{r regress_scan, eval=FALSE}
out_scan1 <- scan1(iron_probs, iron$pheno, Xcovar=Xcovar, kinship=k,
                   cores=0)
out_regress <- scan1gen(iron_probs, iron$pheno, Xcovar=Xcovar,
                        func=reg, kinship=k, cores=0)
out_scan1_loco <- scan1(iron_probs, iron$pheno, Xcovar=Xcovar,
                        kinship=k_loco, cores=0)
out_regress_loco <- scan1gen(iron_probs, iron$pheno, Xcovar=Xcovar,
                             func=reg, kinship=k_loco, cores=0)
```

```{r regress_scan_really, echo=FALSE}
file <- "scan1gen_cache/regress.RData"
if(!file.exists(file)) {
<<regress_scan>>
    save(out_scan1, out_regress, out_scan1_loco, out_regress_loco, file=file)
} else {
    load(file)
}
```

Here's a plot of the four scans for the liver phenotype:
The `scan1` results are in blue and the regress results are in pink.
Solid curves are with an overall kinship matrix, and dashed curves are
with "loco". The results using regress are hardly different; not using
loco seriously dampens QTL evidence.

```{r plot_regress_scan_liver}
par(mar=c(5.1, 4.1, 0.6, 0.6))
plot(out_scan1, iron_map, ylim=c(0, 7))
plot(out_scan1_loco, iron_map, add=TRUE, lty=2)
plot(out_regress, iron_map, add=TRUE, col="violetred")
plot(out_regress_loco, iron_map, add=TRUE, lty=2, col="violetred")
```

Here are the results for the spleen phenotype, which show similar differences.

```{r plot_regress_scan_spleen}
par(mar=c(5.1, 4.1, 0.6, 0.6))
plot(out_scan1, iron_map, lodcolumn=2, ylim=c(0, 13))
plot(out_scan1_loco, iron_map, lodcolumn=2, add=TRUE, lty=2)
plot(out_regress, iron_map, lodcolumn=2, add=TRUE, col="violetred")
plot(out_regress_loco, iron_map, lodcolumn=2, add=TRUE, lty=2, col="violetred")
```

It's easier to see the differences between the scan1 and regress if we
subtract the LOD scores. Here are the results with the liver phenotype.

```{r plot_regress_scan_differences_liver}
par(mar=c(5.1, 4.1, 0.6, 0.6))
plot(out_regress - out_scan1, iron_map, ylim=c(-0.25, 0.25))
plot(out_regress_loco - out_scan1_loco, iron_map, add=TRUE, lty=2)
```

And here are the LOD differences for the spleen phenotype.

```{r plot_regress_scan_differences_spleen}
par(mar=c(5.1, 4.1, 0.6, 0.6))
plot(out_regress - out_scan1, iron_map, lodcolumn=2, ylim=c(-0.25, 0.25))
plot(out_regress_loco - out_scan1_loco, iron_map, lodcolumn=2, add=TRUE, lty=2)
```


## sommer package

As our final example of `scan`gen`, let's do basically the same thing
as we did with the
[regress](https://cran.r-project.org/package=regress) package, but
using the [sommer](https://cran.r-project.org/package=sommer) package.

Here's the fitting function:

```{r fit_sommer}
fit_sommer <- function(probs, pheno, addcovar=NULL, kinship, ...)
{
    df <- as.data.frame(pheno)
    colnames(df) <- "pheno"

    if(is.null(probs)) {
        formula <- "pheno ~ 1"
    } else {
        df <- cbind(df, probs)
        formula <- paste("pheno ~", paste(colnames(probs), collapse="+"))
    }

    if(!is.null(addcovar)) {
        formula <- paste(formula, "+", paste(colnames(addcovar), collapse="+"))
        df <- cbind(df, addcovar)
    }
    df <- cbind(df, id=rownames(pheno))

    max(sommer::mmes(as.formula(formula), random=~vsm(ism(id), Gu=kinship), data=df,
                     dateWarning=FALSE, verbose=FALSE)$llik)/log(10)
}
```

Let's try using the `grav2` dataset included with R/qtl2. This is for
a set of Arabidopsis recombinant inbred lines, with the outcome being
a [gravitropism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitropism) phenotype.

```{r prep_grav2}
grav2 <- read_cross2(system.file("extdata", "grav2.zip", package="qtl2"))
grav2_map <- insert_pseudomarkers(grav2$gmap, step=1)
grav2_probs <- calc_genoprob(grav2, grav2_map, cores=0)
grav2_k <- calc_kinship(grav2_probs, "loco")
```

We'll first do genome scans for all phenotypes ("tip angle" at
different times), using `scan1`.

```{r grav2_scan1}
out_scan1 <- scan1(grav2_probs, grav2$pheno, kinship=grav2_k, cores=0)
```

Let's pick out just the phenotype with maximum LOD score, and use that
with the sommer package:

```{r max_pheno_grav2}
mxphe <- which.max(apply(out_scan1, 2, max))
```

Now the genome scan with our fitting function that uses sommer:

```{r sommer_scan}
out_sommer <- scan1gen(grav2_probs, grav2$pheno[, mxphe], kinship=grav2_k,
                       func=fit_sommer, cores=0)
```

Here are plots of the two:

```{r plot_grav2_scans}
plot(out_scan1, grav2_map, lodcolumn=mxphe)
plot(out_sommer, grav2_map, add=TRUE, col="violetred")
```

Here is a plot of the differences:

```{r plot_grav2_scans_diff}
plot(out_sommer - out_scan1[,mxphe], grav2_map, ylim=c(-0.2, 0.2))
```

Here is how we would do a permutation test:

```{r perms_sommer, eval=FALSE}
operm_sommer <- scan1perm(grav2_probs, grav2$pheno[,mxphe], kinship=grav2_k,
                          scan_func=scan1gen, func=fit_sommer,
                          n_perm=1000, cores=0)
```

```{r perms_sommer_really, echo=FALSE}
set.seed(20260711)
file <- "scan1gen_cache/perms_sommer.rds"
if(!file.exists(file)) {
<<perms_sommer>>
    saveRDS(operm_sommer, file)
} else {
    operm_sommer <- readRDS(file)
}
```

And here is the 5% significance threshold:

```{r perms_sommer_summary}
summary(operm_sommer)
```

## Treatment of multiple phenotypes

Just like `scan1`, the `scan1gen` function can handle multiple phenotypes.
But we assume the user-supplied fitting function handles just a single
phenotype, and we then call it repeatedly for each of the phenotype
columns.

You could, instead, handle the multiple phenotypes within the fitting
function. In some cases, this can be done with considerable savings in
computation time. The fitting function should then return a vector of
LOD scores, one for each phenotype column, and when you call
`scan1gen`, you should use `vectorize_func=FALSE`.


## Permutation tests with `scan1snps`

As a final example, we'll show how to use `scan1perm` with the
`scan1snps` function: to do permutations when performing a SNP-by-SNP
association scan in a multi-parent population.

We'll consider the Diversity Outcross data from [Gatti et al.
(2014)](https://doi.org/10.1534/g3.114.013748), available at the
[qtl2data
repository](https://github.com/rqtl/qtl2data/tree/main/DO_Gatti2014).

First we load the data:

```{r load_data}
file <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rqtl/qtl2data/main/DO_Gatti2014/do.zip"
do <- read_cross2(file)
```

Let's calculate genotype probabilities and the "loco" kinship.

```{r calc_probs_kinship}
do_gmap <- insert_pseudomarkers(do$gmap, step=1)
do_pmap <- interp_map(do_gmap, do$gmap, do$pmap)
do_probs <- calc_genoprob(do, do_gmap, error_prob=0.002, cores=0)
do_aprobs <- genoprob_to_alleleprob(do_probs, cores=0)
do_k <- calc_kinship(do_aprobs, "loco", cores=0)
```

We'll consider just the WBC phenotype, and we'll use a square-root
transformation.

```{r do_pheno}
do_pheno <- sqrt(do$pheno[,1])
```

To perform a SNP-based genome scan, we need access to a database of
SNPs in the founder lines, available at
[figshare](https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.5280229.v3). We then
use `create_variant_query_func` to create a function that queries the database.

```{r create_query_func}
query_variants <- create_variant_query_func("~/Data/CCdb/cc_variants.sqlite")
```

To perform a SNP association scan, we use `scan1snps()`:

```{r scan1snps, eval=FALSE}
out_snps <- scan1snps(do_aprobs, map=do_pmap, pheno=do_pheno, kinship=do_k,
                      query_func=query_variants, cores=0)
```

```{r scan1snps_really, echo=TRUE}
file <- "scan1gen_cache/out_snps.rds"
if(!file.exists(file)) {
<<scan1snps>>
    saveRDS(out_snps, file)
} else {
    out_snps <- readRDS(file)
}
```

And to do a permutation test, we use `scan1perm` with
`scan_func=scan1snps`:

```{r perm_snps, eval=FALSE}
operm_snps <- scan1perm(do_aprobs, map=do_pmap, pheno=do_pheno, kinship=do_k,
                        scan_func=scan1snps, query_func=query_variants,
                        cores=0, n_perm=1000)
```

Here's the 5% significance threshold:

```{r summary_perm_snps}
summary(operm_snps)
```

---

[![CC BY](https://i.creativecommons.org/l/by/3.0/88x31.png)](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/)
[Karl Broman](https://kbroman.org)
